speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Elvon Hampton took place at his home in Genesee on May 3, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Started farming here. Oh, well, he came out.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: He was a fairly young age. I suppose he was probably about maybe 26, something like that. He came to Dayton over there. It seemed like a lot of the early pioneers came to Dayton. By that time, when he worked there a little while, he told me about fishing up in those creeks up in there. Then, for some reason or other, he moved on further out here. There were several other fellows with him from Carolina. I know we used to go to visit one. I forget his name now. He was up around east of Moscow. Of course, in those days, they wanted to homestead a little bit up in the timber country. They tried that, several of these neighbors and several of his friends did, and probably established a homestead. He said, for some reason or other, he didn't like that. It seemed kind of slow to him, sitting out there in the building and cutting timber. He just wasn't quite adapted to the timber life of some of these other men. Then he migrated, I think, right down here in this area and worked on a farm for a couple of months by the highways to come by. I remember there was a fellow who used to be a greaser out there. He talked about plowing with a foot burner out there for a few years. He happened to travel out east of Genesee and went on to a place out there for sale by an insurance company. It seemed like in those earlier days, the insurance company had had possession of those properties. He said he bought that piece of ground back there without even going back off of the road to look at it. He said, the land looks good from the road. He just purchased that place there then and started building some old buildings on it there. He built up on it and improved on it. Then he just kept gradually, in those days, adding a little more to his holdings there. He was next to the Indian land, the Reservation, the Nez Perce Reservation down there. The Indians could sell their land then. Some of the Indians that owned the property were, well, it wasn't too productive then. They ran a few cattle on it and they weren't too successful, so they were willing to sell to somebody who would cultivate it. It wasn't anything like it is today because they had operated with horses in the original. Later, it was mules that he used there because of the hiring people, as I told you before. Let me ask you this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you why he decided to leave Carolina and why he wanted to come west?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No, I just can't recall. Unless it was that, oh, I think his family wasn't too large. It wasn't on that basis, but I suppose he just was ambitious. It seemed like back there they worked on a smaller scale. Whether he heard about this country, I don't know, but they raised a baccala back there. It was a hard task and a menial job and a manual labor. Outside of that, I just don't really know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he probably had the idea of owning his own spread and not the problem of labor?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I think so. I think that he was that type of a man. I don't know what happened. He played a banjo. He had a banjo on. Over here at the end of the fire.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: What?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And I see over there at the end of the fire. Oh, yeah. And I kept that. He used to play, you know, at rainy days. I never learned how to play that, but there's his old banjo. And you can see, and it was a five string banjo. And of course, I have gone up, I've been musical. I went to the singing route. And you can see where he just picked it. He never did use a pick. I got this pick here to do it, but there's a five string banjo and he picked that. And I remember, well, I guess before I knew about it, he probably played out the dances and things like that. And whether he'd learned to play that back home, I don't know. It wouldn't surprise me because it was an instrument. They played it out. You know, more than I knew. He could sit and play these old tunes, you know, Joe Clark. And I remember sitting around the coastal with home out there at the ranch. And it was rainy days. He had to get out this banjo and play it. And he loved that. And he always loved music. And I took up the singing, and I did a lot of singing and I still do and did that in my life. And we had a lot of it. I refurbished it and fixed it up. And that's the old value. Oh, that's nice. That's a nice looking instrument. It's an old instrument.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, about when do you think he landed here?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, I think in about 1886 or 1989, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was no homestead land that he had around Dead Sea by then?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No. No, it was up in the timberland. And you had to go down here and purchase it. And it was hard to then, when you acquired your properties then, it was difficult to work it. Because you had to travel and didn't have, you know, we didn't have the mobility that we have today. It's hard for it to visualize that. With halter and grain or the wagon to town. And in my town, it amazed me that I could think back that we did that. We traveled by sled. And those early days that I never got to town right now. But we got to town, you know, on the 4th of July or some space of time. Hardly ever went out for lunch anyplace. That would be something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why would that be?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, we didn't have any. We weren't so busy then, those days. Unless you went for a purpose then. If you took your surrey and your team, you went to town to provide groceries. Or you actually took your, in the fall, you took your wheat to grind it at a flour mill. Or, they had one down here. And so in those days, you went for a purpose. You didn't say, well, we'll just go in and get the mail. Or we're going to get a quart of milk. You didn't have to get milk then because you had milk cows. I milked a cow even after I was married in my family.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And we butchered our hogs, our pork. We entered bacon and cured all of that and made our lard and made the soap. And there was a lady down here, Mrs. Kern, used to come out. And I couldn't see her in the yard yet. We'd put a fork and hold her up and put this big ground pot in there and boil that lard and stir it out there in the wind. Stir it, you know, on its harden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would come over to make the soap?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah. She was the woman who lived in town here, a German lady. And she knew how to do that just in so easily. You know, I'd get her to come out and she would take charge of that. And she was a specialist, you might say. And then we'd cut this meat up, you know, and had no place, no refrigeration and no place in town. We had to cure it and can your meat. And then in the summertime when we needed meat, we'd kill lamb. We had sheep. We'd kill lamb whenever we needed some fresh, not to eat it. We'd maybe kill a beef. It'd cure out in the cool of the night, you know. Of course the hogs we butchered in the wintertime. When it was cold. Oh, we used to butcher it doesn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More hogs than what? Really?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, of course we had men in the summertime, caught a few men. And it took a lot of food, a lot of baking, baked all the bread, you know. When I talk about that, I think our fellow doesn't really, really, the guy is talking through his hat, you know, here in this generation. But when you're thinking, I often recall how I think back how this change has come. And I told you about something here last time I was here. And there's a picture of those mules and their combine. And that's in 31. Well, 31 was, that was two years before I graduated out of college.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And I was driving a team there. And then you see how we progressed when we had this. And then this thing back here, you see, we started with a tractor. They had a tractor that was a smaller one. And we just experimented with that. And then eventually, here a little later the last year, after this one, I think in 33, where we used six mules back here, and we put this tractor up in front. And we cut off all these mules up here we're trying to cut off. You know, we're trying to get changed over to a mechanized system.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you explain to me again why it was that your father preferred the mules to the horses?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: He was in the minority opinion around this country. Oh, yes. I don't suppose there were too many. I can't think of too many more, except there used to be a fella up in the voids up around Moscow. We're big operators. And then he had to be bigger operators.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And he did that because I think when he, I have some pictures of horses down there, that the casualty rate in horses was pretty high because they're more of a personalized animal. You have to look after him. You have to not get him overheated or watch their food, for instance, they'll found her. Whereas this mule won't do that. He will take care of himself. If he's too hot when he comes in and you've been working him hard in the field, he won't drink water. And when you take him out to the barn, he won't, he'll never have to find a mule to the founder. He won't just jump in and eat all that grain that he got there. He'll ration himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did this have to do with his thinking that with a lot of hired men, it would be easier to look after him?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, that's where, you know, he just couldn't be in out there every day and caution a man. He'd hire a man and say, there's your six or seven mules, you go up there and harness them. And there's the grain, we'd have a bulk grain in the bin and go feed those, you know. Instead of, you know, how a man would come in, if he's a careless man, he would come in and take a bucket and maybe dump a couple of gallons instead of one gallon and one box and maybe not that so much in the end. Well, if he did that to him, he wouldn't be sick the next morning. And I imagine he found out when that happened the next morning when the men went up there to work, he'd probably say, well, here, I got a sick horse. And then, well, that probably held up his unit all day. And it was quite a problem. And then not only horses were, they were temperamental.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Horses?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I remember him telling about nice, pretty big horses and they would come in here and trade during the week maybe and they're bulky. You'd get a nice big horse, you know, what they call a cold -shouldered horse and he just wouldn't hit that collar. And the fella, he'd come in and trade that horse off and he'd think he was a nice one. The next morning, or the next Monday morning when he hooked him up to leave town or something, he never had a load for that horse wooden foil. And there he would be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's cold -shouldered?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, he just wouldn't hit that collar, you know. They'd just have it. They'd put a big old collar on him, you know, and what they call him cold -shouldered, he just wouldn't hit. He'd just back up, he'd just hit that little bit and just back right up in the traces. I've seen him do that. Pretty great big horses. Just what they call their bulky. So you'd trade for a horse then. You were taking chances. So lots of time my dad would tell about how he'd come in. He'd get stuck, you know, and the next weekend he'd have to wait until the next week maybe. He'd have to substitute some way, or he'd bring this nice pretty horse back, you know, and for those to come by, and they'd probably be the same spot as if he was, but nobody knew what the matter. So they'd trade horses. That's what the horse traded, you know. That's what the horse traded, that's their name. And they would liquor on him and trade, and he'd go back to smiling and say, well, that fella hooked that horse up, he's going to be a drunk. So it was a cause.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think this had something to do with why he preferred mules?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, that's why they were more reliable. And the less there is, and you know, they had a man to work with him, and he couldn't go buy a horse or replace him. We generally had two or three mules who were extras, you know, or to get hurt or something else. And very seldom would you. And another thing that they're not a nervous type of animal, and if they ever get in a wire fence, just stand there, so you'll come and get them. And a horse will just tear himself apart. See, no horse get in there and just get inside and just cut his legs off on a barbed wire fence. So you can see the advantage of that kind of animal. I take it that he started himself with horses and decided to turn the mule. Yeah, I remember when they had horses, and he had these horses. I could show you a picture down there of all those horses lined out, or he supported using them. But he switched pretty early in my life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I was a young boy then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where he went for his mule supply?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, I remember he would go. You see, you have to breed those, you know. They won't reproduce. Mules won't reproduce, and you have to breed a jack with a horse. And there were fellows that would raise those. Like I said, these boys up here who did that, and they had kept a bunch of brood mares. And they would, I remember going, I remember one time we went, I don't know whether you went to Montana or someplace, and we bought a bunch of, year in there, two -year -old, pretty mules. And my dad left me there with another hired man to tie, learn to tie these up, you know, and they were pretty. They had some bay ones, they were pretty, they were different colors, you know. Generally they were brown. But you see, there's some white ones there. And they were pretty fellows, and we had a lot of fun tying those things up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, listen, did he get ribbed about having mules instead of horses by guys around here?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: It's not unusual. No, no. No, I don't think anybody ever did that because they knew it was the advantage of it. But then, of course, you take a fellow, if he was an individual farmer, and he took pride in his horses and watched them and took care of them, he could get by a lot better, you know, because he knew just how much to feed them, any particular horse. But where you had five or six fellows working, why, you know, and maybe one man at Levy or somebody else, you had breaking ends. Of course, it didn't change too much in those days. If a man was a mule skinner, what they call a mule skinner, that's why he stayed for maybe a year after year. Well, I'd like to talk about that some. Maybe first, but I'd like to talk about the operation itself, but first maybe you tell me how he went from being a small farmer to a pretty good -sized operator. Really, the way he opened it up. And there's my dad standing on the stack pile. You see, that's when he used to have those thrashing machines. That's where he comes. Now, here's the one out at the place where they had these mules. See, they'd all come in here to drink, and here's the fellows that's lined up there, they're working in this place. And here's how to be a picture of the crew that summer. It shows you there. Yeah, there's my dad. There I am, my brother. And here's a fellow that worked for us for many, many years.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And here's what I say when they had these horses. They would have colts and stuff. Now, that's in 1904. You see, there'd be seven, I think there's six or seven to a team, six, I guess. And here's my homestead, the original. I mean, where I lived and I lived in that house, I was born in that house. Here's a picture of a combine that I took when I had to later on with the tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the means by which he built up his place?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, he was just... He was quite a trader, and then he raised livestock. He would have sheep, we had quite a few sheep, hogs and everything, you know, self -sufficient, you know. And grain, you know, wasn't too productive a thing. The yield wasn't too high then. And he was a good manager. He just... whatever the land produced, and he protected that and was able to come out, you know. We didn't let what lay away a lot of money. And of course, property wasn't as high then. You didn't have a big cash flow. And just about the only... you just didn't buy gasoline or stuff like that. You just put it in the... what little labor you had there. And lots of times he would provide, you know... I've known some fellows that would just come out there and work for their board, chop wood, and work for the wintertime, maybe, and keep the place together while we were in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he save up enough money to put it down on more land and buy more? Did he expand that way?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, he would save a little, and he would always borrow at the bank down here. There was a fellow named Bresler, who was a good banker, and he would stay with him. And he would go... he would loan him the money, lots of time putting all of it. And maybe he'd have a crop of wheat or barley. But like I say, the cash flow wasn't very great. When your expense was great, you had a lot of labor. You had to handle it with sacks and soam and haul them. It was slow, but what you did was comparable, more so than it is today, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he mind being in debt and being extended on?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No. No, I guess he saw the opportunity was great, and people weren't too anxious. It was hard to have a crew and horses and stuff like that if you were willing to go on. And the opportunity was there, you know. And I know that in those days you had more land than you wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Exactly, yeah. You just had more land than you wanted. You'd just get it around, nobody came to want it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the Drisco part of the partnership? How did he go into partnership with that?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, he was a man here that had been a schoolteacher, and was a slight type of a man, not a farmer type, but a financier. And he ran a hardware store down here. And he saved his money and looked around, and he went in partnership with a dad to help finance him. And so dad did the operation, he paid him his salary, and he made the living. So even in that early days, he did it from the financial standpoint. And so my dad knew the farming end of it, so he went to partnership just like you. Lots of times you form a partnership.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this mean they had separate land holdings at the same time?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah. When they acquired land, it's like where that team is that was on his place there, you see. Why he would buy a piece of land, he bought a piece of land where that was what they called a Troutman place, and then from then on they added each one. See, he had his place over where this homestead was, see, and he lived there. That's about a mile from there. And he would, when the property would come up, why they would buy, take maybe a half a year, a lady that lay next to them, and next to their property, which would be more convenient, why they would buy it that way. And when the Indian land came up, why my dad, more or less, bought that town, it was down in this brazeria, I don't know, of just because I was buying any of that. Why he didn't, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So once he got going, how much land was he actually farming?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, when he was farming there, they had all around just about 2 ,000 acres. They each had about 1 ,000 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About when would this have been?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, there would have been in the 30s. Well, up to the 30s, 35. And when Drisco died, he died first, while his daughter went in partnership. They had a partnership, and I went in partnership with her. And we farmed for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much of an idea of how many years it took your father to be farming that much? He probably started with, what, 160 acres?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, well, I suppose that they bought that in 1890. I suppose he started in about 1895, and it took him 30, 40 years. To build up to that 2 ,000. But they were doing some kind of partnership by 1900 already, because they had the Thrashen crew as a joint crew.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: So your father, from what you were mentioning before, he looked at himself as a manager. Yeah, there was a picture of him up there, very stern. I never can think of him driving a team. He was always a man who was out managing. I think you should tell him about the Gulf Creek idea, that he told you how to drive. One time when I was young, and we had these push binders, which you drive with six mules, and there's a beam, and it more or less came closer to what these combines are now, because the binder would go ahead of your team, and the cut your reel would cut that, although the bow reel would knock down your wheat. So I was a young fella then, and I remember starting up the road with this team, and if you had a green team, it was a little hard to hold them in a little bit, and he had a rudder, just like a rudder. Well, it reminds me of these outboard motors you could just swing around, but this rudder had a seat on it with a wooden peg in between it, and you'd guide it like this, just like this swing like this. I think you understand what I mean. I don't have a picture of one of those. And so my dad was along the road one day, and I was coming along when I was diving in and out like this, trying to keep my team in, and one thing on the other end, and he's telling me what to do, and a very patient fella though. And finally I jumped off, and I said, well, you take it. And he said, no, son. He says, I don't know how to do it, but he says, I know how it should be done. So I got back on that, and I stayed with it. I thought that was good enough for me. So he was, of course, he had done some earlier work. When he was a young fella, I worked hard, but he was a good manager, good figure. He always threw anything away, and never said anything. Treaties, employees are good, but you're good. And he was always a fair man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of dimensions now, as far as the size of the crew and the amount of stock did he need to farm 2 ,000 acres?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, he just probably needed to, you know, there's lots of times he wouldn't get a cloud or a dun. And the pressure is great, I remember, even in a partnership to produce. I remember now, this just goes with a fella who could come out and take a look at things. He'd take a drive around, inspect and see, well, have you got it seated right, or is it going...he wouldn't know how to seat it, but he would know the end result. By the size of the weak group in the field. I know at one time he would come out, and it would make you kind of nervous after you had been working. And I remember my dad said that he came out and looked at the crop, and he said, it isn't going to make very much. I don't know what he said, 20 foot shoulders or something, you know. And my dad said, he said, I'll buy your share out of that. And he said, that shut him up, he never said anymore. So that's the way he had to get it on.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: So it's not an easy life. A lot of responsibility. He didn't have to... For his own good, but for the good of his partner, and of course of course he would get wages for his partner, Mary Jean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what kind of wages he got?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, it wasn't very much. Maybe he would get $2 ,000 at the most.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the season's worth?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah. Yeah, that would have been a pretty good salary. Those days, I remember we were working, just like those drivers would work for $2, $1 .5 or $2 a day in the furnishing board and room.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And we'd... I don't know how this time would get a little more, but I worked lots of days for $2 .5. I thought that was pretty good. Worked hard too, lots of days, lots of hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of day was it when you were working?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I mean, it was like 10, 12 hours a day. Oh yeah, we would get up at 5 o 'clock and go up to the barn and feed those horses, those mules, and harness them. Lots of times we had lanterns sitting up there. Of course it wouldn't be in the morning, but at night. We were hanging on the peg and we had to light that lantern to feed them at night. But we would go up there and... Then when we got them harnessed and fed, that early in the morning, we'd go down and eat breakfast. We had this lady cook there, you'd see her there. And she cooked for as many, many years. She'd just operate that house. And we would go down there and eat, and then we'd go back and take our teams out and go out in the field, and then come in for 12 at noon and feed them and water them again, put them up in the barn. And then maybe they'd down a little while at noon. Like now, we'd have to go back to work. We'd go down and maybe have a little nap, 10, 15, 20 minutes, to give the team time enough to eat. And then we'd get back in about 6 o 'clock at night. We never worked really late or dark hours, but of course when the fall would come, it'd get darker quicker. But we kept regular hours. When we had to crew, we had to keep regular hours. Be in the meal time so the cooks would know when the men would be in, not scraggling in. Very disciplined program. And you know what they expected of everybody. That's the way he operated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard would you consider the work was? Would men, would you be working about really up close to your limit, or was it really easy?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh no, no it wasn't. When you're a plow, understanding, it would be a tiresome job. Or sometimes when you're hay, it would be a more difficult job. It's nothing slavery about it. Gosh, no, I felt I could enjoy it. Everybody enjoyed it. It was a job.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: But it took a lot of time. You just couldn't hurry to think you had so much time. That's why you had to be out there, to go around and get around us in those fields and get that much work done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you call for a lot of teamwork on the part of the crew, or was it more?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh yeah, it's like you saw there were those horses there. There would be a man, the fella would be the leader. And it was up his responsibility to get out there and get the teams to go and see. It's like the fella who is the head one. Maybe the guy would sit around and lay around a little bit, but he's got to jump right out to the bunkhouse. We'd sleep in the bunkhouse. And what we called the bunkhouse for the men. He would have to get out, and he would have to be the first one out there. That depends on him. Sometimes you'd pay him a little more for being the leader, sitting down out there or something. So he had incentives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you usually do in the harvest?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Did you have one job you kind of stuck to, or did... I worked with this older fella, like I tell you there. We put up hay. I was a little bit too young, I guess, to be out shocking. That's when we had shocking. And then I finally graduated when I was smaller. I carried water with the team through mules and a bucket to the shockers. We'd had maybe 15 shockers before we had a combine. But of course when we got up to the combine stage, of course that was practically late, you might say, up in the 28th, 29th. But before that, from 1918 on up in there, I did a minio job where I drove a deer ache on a haystacker. And actually that was hard as a man's job, but that was a kid's job. A man wouldn't hardly do that. You know, a guy's tedious, backing the team mule up, just backing them up, backing them up. You just wear the grass out, you know, where you do that. And I knew I used to heat that version of anything else because of the tedious job, but I stayed with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you were a kid? You were expected to do that?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, it was a light enough job, but a tedious one. I couldn't handle a fork or something like that, but I could do that. Now it'd be automated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you first started to work in a farming?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, I started then, of course, before that I herded sheep, herded cattle. Did anything like that when you're old enough to ride a horse. And, gosh, and then of course automation started maybe in the 20s. And of course I learned to drive an old Ford truck on about those times. You know, just barely could look over the dashboard. But there was always work. I could rake. You could rake with a team. I remember I used to rake hay. And you could work in the hay. We did a lot of haying then. We put in about 30 days of hay because we had to provide all the feed to build a barn to feed those animals all summer and winter. And it just took a lot of that. And it did chores, milk cows, feed pigs. They always had pigs, petting animals. So, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea when you were a kid that your father had about paying you for the work that you did? When did you start getting paid for farming?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, he always paid us then. We just didn't work. For nothing. He always had an incentive. I worked, oh, I would work in the summertime and have my own money here and when I went to school I had my own account. And he didn't expect us to provide for the system. Of course, we were in a different family thing. We were in a partnership. We provided work for this other family.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: So, naturally I do work for my family, but then why should I work for the other family, you see?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: For the family then. For your family, that was more choral. Yeah, so that's why when you had it in a partnership, why everybody had to participate in some way and had to be an expense out of it. So, gardens and stuff like that on a home garden, you know, would mean nothing, you know. But, Hona is old enough to go out there and take a job and do something along with the farming operation.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: It was paid for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men did he usually have working here?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I'm sure it varied from season to season. Oh, of course it varied. Taking the spring, there would be five or six men. And it must be in the fall. And of course during harvest, before we got into harvesting, when we were thrashing, I remember, well then I would take 15 or 20 men for 25 or 30 days. But then when we got into it, like when we got into that combine then, well then we would have about 10 men. But for the steam thrashing, they had their own outfit that they used. Of course that was contracted. Well that was contracted. Yeah, he didn't own that. The owner of the front of it would go to the thrashing and they would just do that. They would move because you couldn't have that big outfit and just go and do 10, 20 days. Moved another place 10, 20 days and they did that on a contract basis. And they would provide everything and that was their management problem. Once you got the, you had to bind the wheat and shock it, put it in the shock, then the thrasher would come down and pick it up and everything else. And put it in that pile, even that sack pile. And then when it was sacked, we'd haul it to town. And so they brought in all their own men to do that. Yeah, they had their own crew. So the crew was on your place, it wasn't that big then. No, no after that you see. The owner for your shockings and stuff, but they wouldn't have it. And they would have, oh a thrashing crew like that would probably be 20 men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well for the crews that were working for your father, how long would those men be employed? Would they be there from the spring till the fall or how would that work?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh yeah, they'd be there at the spring and they'd be there on the fall. They'd keep those fellows there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This would be maybe 8, 10 men?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, well not quite that many. I'd say at least 6 maybe. You know, he'd have 6 or 17, you know, like that. I don't think we had any more than that. But it went down. And of course, in the wintertime there wasn't too much to do. I think those fellows, I don't know, some of them would go home I guess or live with their families. I know they wouldn't have too many in the wintertime. Two or three men maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these be local men or would they be?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: They would be local. All those longer term men would be local. The other men, like when you had these shocking stuff, they would be transients. They would be fellows that would come in here on the train, just like apple pickers that come down in apple countries. And it worked. And that's the one you'd have problems. You may take a man, that's when he'd be busy. He may come in and pick up a man and take him out and maybe just take another man like the town. It wouldn't work or it wouldn't be good or something. He'd be alcoholic or stuff like that. And they'd run into all those things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it usually be different men that your father would pick up every year or would he get the same guys come in here?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No, they would generally be different men. This is not these transients. Yeah, they would probably never get back in here, unless he was a specialized man. If he was a sax or if he was a specialized, if he was just an ordinary laborer, you could never depend on a repeat.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: So I take it that made some of the biggest headaches that he would have during his years. Yeah, it was a great problem then. Yeah, you just never knew whether they would produce for you or not.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And some fellas would maybe just come out and get a good meal and quit. That sounds like the old camp inspectors and the logging camps. They used to call those guys who'd go from camp to camp for a meal and a place to sleep.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: You would find these guys right in town. That's where you'd pick them up. Yeah, they used what they called a jungle down here where this cow creek runs down there. There's a cross -road bridge down there. I crossed there every time I'd go out to my place. The train would come in there, the depot there would come in. These fellas would come in there and they'd camp in there. They would blank it down, have a pot. Some guys would have something to eat. There'd be somebody I can remember as a kid that'd be somebody in there, but all the time. Maybe it's a camp tender. And we'd pick those people up right there. You'd have to be a judge of a man to go in there. And you'd pick some good men. Some wouldn't be worth it. He would just talk to him one individual one at a time and say... Yeah, I'd have to interview him himself there. That's the only way you could employ people.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: I take it there was a big difference between these fellas and the guys that were regular hired. They were trained family men, I might say. I can remember. Some of those people would come from back to Carolina. Some of those fellas, boys that came out here, he gave them jobs and they worked here. And they eventually started farming for themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they knew when they came out that he was out here?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh yeah, I think they knew he was here. In fact, quite a few of these fellas were from back there. There's that fella. One, two, three, four, five, six. That was six guys from back there in Carolina. This old fella was a fella that came back quite a few years. He was a good mule driver. He drove the combine, four I ever drove. This fella was a sack sewer.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, that's real interesting. So in that way, he was helping these fellas to get a start. They were coming out here and wanting to get a start. I can't think of those. As far as it went, farm this and other fellas before that. Do you think that for these guys that were doing that work, from what I understand about it, working as a hired man was certainly less desirable for a man than being able to farm his own place would be if he could do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering, do you think that's what most of these people had in mind?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, no. They came out here and some of those fellas went back to Carolina. Most of those that maybe wanted to. They wanted to see what the West was like and come out here. My dad was patient. He would train them and they could go work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these fellas be there a year after a year?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Just a summer. Most of those fellas just stayed there one summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say in general that his hired hands were a different group each year?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, pretty much so. Yeah, they are. Like I say, those fellas, there's five or six of those that didn't come back. A couple of them stayed over the winter. Of course, in the wintertime, we didn't have anything to do. We just feed them. Of course, we didn't feed them. Didn't pay anything. Just keep them maybe a month or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how was it, your idea of what it was for him? I mean, you have to, if you're performing and in charge of the men, have good relations with him, did he find it easy to have everybody working together and them doing what he wanted them to do?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, yes, he really didn't. Outside of a leader, he never had a foreman per se. He was a foreman. You know, but he would have, for instance, if you had one leader, like I said there, if he wanted to get the team outside of that, he'd give every man his job. And he'd always be available out there and operating. He did the foreman work. He didn't delegate outside of a temporary leadership.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess what I'm thinking is how well do you think that those guys would get along, would they do just what he would want them to do?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, yeah, yeah, there was no problem. Of course, you just couldn't find jobs, you know, to have a funny one. Didn't make it too tough, made it just as pleasant as possible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had the job to do, and there wasn't any arm twisting, you know?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: You know, and when you get in with the group, and of course, you can see there, he had younger fellows that are capable and willing to do, or willing to learn, you know. It wasn't those days that you couldn't fall back on food stamps or welfare or nothing else. You didn't make that, you didn't get a favor back home. I'm glad that wasn't held over you. But you always had pride in their work then. That's what I said, you know, the one that you knew when you had a longer time. So, like I say, some of these other tranche of people, that was a different problem.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And it wasn't quite so widespread, because, but you were busy during those times. Of course, it seemed like we got over those periods in a few years. We started in the war times, 1918, on up to 20, for 10 or 12 years in there. It was pretty tough.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Do you, you know, they said that, I know in logging camps during World War I, they had, there was a lot of trouble with the strikes, and the IWW was in the camps, and there was some concern for a while at that time, that they were going to try to do some things in the fields too, in the farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about that?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, yes, we had. They were, IWWs, they talked about those there, down in here, and they were concerned about that at the time, you know, about fires. But it never got, it never got to the point where it was dangerous. But I remember them talking about that. I'm concerned about fellas, and actually, if you go down here to the jungle and pick up a guy, if he is satisfied or something, he could drop a match out there and burn you up. And so, so he, if a fellow went out and he would eat, maybe you'd give him a meal, and you'd probably be mad about it, and he wanted to go back to town, you'd better take him, to let him sit around the bunkhouse, and make him walk, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he have to do a lot of logistics to, was all this land together, or was it spread out enough?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No, it's pretty well spread out. Lots of times we'd have to move our feeding racks three or four miles down, way down there, I remember, having those set up way down again to Kelly Gulch, and oh, not three or four miles, but at least a mile and a half, and you couldn't afford to go back up to the headquarters. And sometimes we'd even, oh, it didn't seem like we took lunches in those times, but they would try to transport some food, but it seemed like we'd maybe ride back up there. Well, we couldn't take the team. We'd hook them up to their place and feed them there and water them. I remember, I remember several places we had like that.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: And it took a lot of management. It took a fellow almost to wait on them, you know, have their feed and have hay and have these wagon boxes there, and you just had to be around all the time. He would be riding around in a buggy or a horse, almost all the time, be available.

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Because if a man wasn't working right, he had to have his operation going. That's what I say it took management, and it took a lot of fortitude to do that. You could just waste the whole thing away. There's no question about it. If you didn't get your crop in, you didn't take care of your livestock, you didn't use your men efficiently and was careless. I think lots of others went by the wayside. Of course, there were not too many people who were operating a unit like he was. They had maybe 160 acres. It was a family unit. A man went out and worked. His children worked. They worked at a garden. They had a few pigs. They had a cow, and there was lots of that, you see. Actually, that's why his system was a little more unique. It was large enough that you had that kind of operation. People looked at that to be a big farm. In those days, it must have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say it was one of the very biggest around the country?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, it was one of the largest in this area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know of anybody that was farming more than 2 ,000 acres?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: No, I don't. I think that was about the largest farm operation around then. He was having to work in a whole different dimension.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were mentioning to me a little before about the hog part of the operation, would you describe that to me again?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: You said you actually went over to Allowa County to pick up the hogs. Oh, yeah. In those days, they didn't have the marketing program that they have now, like an auction. You'd go down to Lewiston and sell and buy, and people would come in. In those days, it's just like I said, he was a trader, like any horses, and he would go out here at the farm and find out some way, maybe a fellow would have, and he would buy his pigs and set a price on them. Even we'd buy calves. Some thought it didn't happen. It didn't have a point to sell. Then we went over into, like I say, when we wanted to get a few more, we got into raising peas, and you had quite a bit of waste, and then you wasted your crops because you didn't have the mechanization now. You had to waste, so you had to pick that up through livestock. We had fence, fence to hog, to all types of fences to hold sheep and hogs in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So we would go over, and I remember going way over into the enterprise area, over above Anatole and Soughton, and buy pigs over there back in that country because those people could raise pigs, and they didn't have the grains back in there to feed them out, see?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: They could keep a sow and feed them and keep that down, but raised the pigs from this. Of course, they had the manpower to do it. The weather was pretty nice back in there. Over here it was colder, and you'd lose your pigs in order time, so we'd go back there and buy off those pigs, and the farmers would sell them to us, and we'd bring them back here and fatten them out because we had the waste wheat and peas and what we'd harvested and some damage and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had a truck over there?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah, that was later when we had the earliest kind of a truck. It was a small Ford truck, nothing like they got to these days. We had double deck sometimes back there. I remember one time we were back there, and there were so many pigs available that we built double deck there with a farmer way back there in the enterprise area. We stayed all night with him. We'd go back there and stay all night with the fella.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many would you make on it? Would you brought back on a trip like that?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: We would get on maybe 50, 60, all day, more than that sometimes. I remember going up here to Colosack down here to Lafayette. You'd go up there to the little place of Colosack up in there. I remember going up there as a boy, was a fella, and we brought pigs back there before they had electric lights up there. They had gas lights in that little hotel we stayed in, the hotel at night. We'd buy pigs and gather them up. We'd buy them. Then, of course, if we were that far, we would go back the next day and buy enough when we didn't know how many we had. We'd go back and pick them up and bring them home the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were they usually when you picked them up?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, they would be about six weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would you keep them?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, we'd have to keep them until that would be spring phase. We would probably have to fatten them out in the fall. It had to be six months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get them in the spring, Selman?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Yeah. They were probably born maybe six weeks. They would be two months, you know, and we'd keep them four months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd be turning them out then as soon as you got them on what was left for the break?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Well, no, we'd feed them a while then. We used to get buttermilk at the dairy down in Lewiston and feed them along there and nurse them up a little. To get them up to what they called feeder pigs. They'd get 60, 70 pounds, and then we could turn them out and they could rustle. They couldn't rustle that young. You'd have to get them up to what they called shoats, feeder pigs. Now, so there was a lot of waste then for them to get. Yeah, you would have to figure that it'd be 10, 15, sometimes 20 percent. Because you had your roads and you had this machinery that you couldn't get down stuff. If you had some down stuff, you had high straw then, and you couldn't pick it up with a mechanized system you would have now, you'd set run over it. And peas would, when we were mowing peas, we'd shatter them, and you had to throw them again on the fork. And so you just had to have that much to pick up. If you didn't pick it up, if you didn't turn them to meat and your animals, why it was lost. So that was quite a complete program that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the sheep part of it?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: Oh, the sheep, of course, you'd shear those sheep. I remember shearing the fellas would come and we'd shear those sheep. And we had the highest, a thousand head of sheep one time. And they would live off of, so we used to summer fall, what they called a summer fall. And there would be a lot of growth in the summertime on that. And these animals would pick that off, tumbling weeds and all kinds of weeds. They would pick that up instead of spraying. We had to do that sheep, you know. And we could put them on wheat two or fifths got a little big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If it got what?

speaker: Elvon Hampton
words: If it got a little too big and all, the foliage got too heavy. Well, we'd trim it down, we'd pasture it down and do that in the spring. And it makes it stool out a little better. And we'd keep those sheep there, and we'd put the hay, put up hay. And we'd feed the sheep. And she would like them. There was enough feed to keep those.
